claim.content: Numerical derivatives of finite-N DMC energy bands are unstable at k≈k_F: the derivative (dE/dk) evaluated from discrete finite-N DMC data often exhibits outlier points in the immediate vicinity of the Fermi surface, a pathology attributable to the combination of (i) the divergence or pathological behavior of the Hartree-Fock band derivative at k_F and (ii) DMC in a finite cell retrieving only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy when using a fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function; consequently, robust estimates of the quasiparticle effective mass are obtained by fitting the band over a finite wave-number range Δk about k_F (using, for example, the quartic parametrization E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4) and using the fit-derived derivative at k_F to average over and suppress the near-k_F anomalies.

conclusion.content: Numerical derivatives of finite-N DMC energy bands are unstable at k≈k_F: the derivative (dE/dk) evaluated from discrete finite-N DMC data often exhibits outlier points in the immediate vicinity of the Fermi surface, a pathology attributable to the combination of (i) the divergence or pathological behavior of the Hartree-Fock band derivative at k_F and (ii) DMC in a finite cell retrieving only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy when using a fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function; consequently, robust estimates of the quasiparticle effective mass are obtained by fitting the band over a finite wave-number range Δk about k_F (using, for example, the quartic parametrization E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4) and using the fit-derived derivative at k_F to average over and suppress the near-k_F anomalies.

premises.content: In diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations of finite periodic simulation cells using fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions, the DMC total-energy estimator recovers only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy in a finite cell; combined with the fact that the derivative of the Hartree-Fock single-particle band with respect to k is divergent or pathological at the Fermi wave vector k_F, this incomplete recovery of correlation energy in a finite cell leads to locally anomalous or outlier numerical estimates of the band derivative (dE/dk) at k≈k_F when derivatives are computed from discrete finite-N DMC data, producing the observed unstable near-k_F derivative points.

steps.reasoning: Define the numerical derivative $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)$ as evaluated from discrete DMC energy-band data using a central-difference approximation; note that the Landau energy functional requires the energy band to be differentiable at the Fermi surface in the infinite system if a quasiparticle effective mass is well defined.

Report the empirical observation from computed numerical derivatives and from derivatives of fitted quartic bands: the numerical derivatives evaluated at points in the immediate vicinity of the Fermi surface $k\approx k_{F}$ are "outliers" (i.e., they depart markedly from the smooth derivative behavior observed away from $k_{F}$), as displayed in the plotted derivatives for paramagnetic and ferromagnetic HEGs; these outliers indicate that the derivative at $k_{F}$ in finite cells is unstable.
Fig. 5
Fig. 6

State the authors' attribution of the pathology: the divergence of the derivative of the Hartree-Fock band at the Fermi surface, combined with the fact that the DMC method retrieves only a finite fraction of the correlation energy in finite cells, leads to a QMC band derivative near $k_{F}$ that exhibits unphysical divergent-like behavior in the finite-cell data; the paper references its earlier discussion of this mechanism and its masking of theoretical $N^{-1/4}$ finite-size behavior.
Ref. 20
Ref. 23

Recommend the robust fitting strategy based on the observed pathology: obtain estimates of the quasiparticle effective mass by fitting the energy band over a finite range $\Delta k$ about $k_{F}$ (i.e., using band data from $k\in[k_{F}-\Delta k,k_{F}+\Delta k]$ with $\Delta k$ not extremely small) to the quartic parametrization $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\alpha_{4}k^{4}$ and then compute $m^{*}$ from the derivative of that fitted polynomial at $k_{F}$; this approach averages over the near-$k_{F}$ anomalies and yields robust per-$N$ estimates that can be more reliably extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit.
Fig. 3
Fig. 4

motivating_questions.content: Fermi-liquid theory requires a well-defined derivative $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)$ at the Fermi surface in the thermodynamic limit, but finite-$N$ calculations sample discrete $k$-points and inherit Hartree-Fock-like divergences that render direct finite-difference derivatives at $k_{F}$ unreliable. Without a practical strategy to mitigate near-$k_{F}$ outliers, computed $m^{*}$ would be unstable and sensitive to which discrete $k$-points were available. Therefore a prescription that (i) recognizes the pathologies and (ii) fits the band over a non-infinitesimal $\Delta k$ to extract a smooth derivative is necessary for stable $m^{*}$ estimation.
